title: Removing a Hidden Round Trip from a Multi-Region AWS API
gist: An AWS engineer describes migrating a multi-region user-settings service from SigV4 (region-pinned request signing, requiring a pre-flight region-discovery round trip) to SigV4a (region-set signing, letting infrastructure route requests), a change triggered not by latency concerns but by repeated us-east-1 outages that left clients cryptographically stuck on a failed region; the code change was small but the six-month multi-team rollout was the hard part.

themes.theme: SigV4's region-pinning constraint and its hidden cost

insights.claim: AWS SigV4 signatures are cryptographically bound to a specific region embedded in the credential scope during signing, so a request signed for us-west-2 is invalid if it arrives in eu-west-1, forcing clients to commit to a destination region before constructing a valid request even when infrastructure (e.g. Route 53 latency-based routing) is designed to make that decision dynamically.
insights.speaker: Suresh Gururajan
insights.so_what: Explains a structural authentication constraint that forces architectural workarounds in any AWS multi-region service using IAM-authenticated API Gateway.
insights.locator: SigV4, the authentication scheme used for IAM-secured API Gateway requests

insights.claim: To work around SigV4's region binding, the team built a pre-flight, unauthenticated DiscoverRegion endpoint that clients called to learn the closest region before signing and sending the actual request, meaning every new client session required two round trips to complete one logical operation.
insights.speaker: Suresh Gururajan
insights.so_what: A concrete pattern -- pre-flight region discovery purely to satisfy an authentication constraint -- that the author later identifies as the generalizable signal for when SigV4a is worth adopting.
insights.locator: We implemented a pre-flight region discovery step as a workaround

insights.claim: At the 90th percentile, the discovery round trip added 75-100ms when the client was in the same region as the discovered endpoint, ~315ms when the endpoint was moderately distant (e.g. us-west-2 to us-east-1), and up to 1 second in the worst case (us-west-2 to ap-south-1/Mumbai).
insights.speaker: Suresh Gururajan
insights.so_what: Quantifies the latency tax of authentication-driven region discovery, useful as a benchmark for evaluating similar workarounds in other multi-region services.
insights.locator: At the 90th percentile (p90), the DiscoverEndpoint latency

themes.theme: Outages, not latency, drove the migration

insights.claim: A series of us-east-1 outages (networking in 12/2021, Lambda in 06/2023, DynamoDB in 10/2025) directly affected the service, and because clients had already completed region discovery and signed requests for us-east-1, their signatures were cryptographically invalid if rerouted elsewhere, forcing a full re-run of the discovery flow to recover -- and it was this resiliency failure, not the latency, that triggered the Q4 2025 re-architecture.
insights.speaker: Suresh Gururajan
insights.so_what: A real-world case study showing how an authentication design choice, not infrastructure, was the actual blocker to regional failover during major cloud outages -- relevant to any team assessing multi-region resilience beyond the infra layer.
insights.locator: These events pushed us to re-architect our settings service

insights.claim: SigV4a had been generally available since Q3 2021, but the team had no reason to adopt it until the 2025 outages made the discovery step's region-pinning an active obstacle to global failover -- illustrating that architectural debt often persists until a forcing incident, not proactive review, surfaces it.
insights.speaker: Suresh Gururajan
insights.so_what: A reminder that periodically re-evaluating old cloud workarounds against newer available capabilities can preempt costly reactive migrations.
insights.locator: Asymmetric Signature Version 4 (SigV4a) had been available for a while

insights.claim: SigV4a uses ECDSA-P256 asymmetric signing instead of HMAC-SHA256, allowing a single signature to be valid for a declared set of regions rather than one region, so the client no longer needs to know its destination region before signing -- infrastructure (Route 53, Global Accelerator, or CloudFront) resolves the actual endpoint and the request stays valid regardless of which region serves it.
insights.speaker: Suresh Gururajan
insights.so_what: Core mechanism explanation for engineers evaluating whether SigV4a can eliminate similar region-discovery workarounds in their own multi-region AWS services.

insights.claim: The region set in SigV4a is a flexible declaration -- it can be as narrow as two adjacent regions for active-passive failover, scoped to a geography (e.g. all EU regions) for data-residency requirements, or expressed with wildcards like us-west-* -- giving fine-grained control over blast radius and compliance scope.
insights.speaker: Suresh Gururajan
insights.so_what: Shows SigV4a can double as a data-residency enforcement mechanism, not just a latency/failover tool.
insights.locator: The region set is just a list

themes.theme: Migration economics: small code change, large coordination cost

insights.claim: The signing-library code change from SigV4 to SigV4a was small -- swapping a single region string for a region-set array -- but the rollout across dependent internal teams took roughly six months end-to-end: about three months to build and deploy the new SigV4a endpoint and three more to run a parallel migration period and complete caller migration.
insights.speaker: Suresh Gururajan
insights.so_what: A concrete benchmark for estimating coordination costs of internal API-authentication migrations -- the technical change is rarely the bottleneck; caller migration is.
insights.quote: The technical change was small. The coordination cost was not.

insights.claim: Rollout issues were process-oriented rather than algorithmic: some corporate firewalls allowlisted only the old domain and silently dropped requests to the new endpoint, some teams had bypassed the vendored SDK with custom signing logic requiring direct outreach, and some clients were stuck on SDK versions too old to support SigV4a without unrelated refactoring first.
insights.speaker: Suresh Gururajan
insights.so_what: A reusable checklist of real-world failure modes for any large-scale internal API/auth migration campaign.
insights.quote: The rollout issues were process-oriented, not algorithmic.
insights.locator: First, some customer environments had corporate firewalls

themes.theme: When SigV4a is (and isn't) the right choice

insights.claim: SigV4a support is narrower than expected: S3 supports it only on Multi-Region Access Points (not standard regional endpoints), and API Gateway REST APIs with IAM auth support it while HTTP APIs do not -- and the failure mode when it's unsupported is an uninformative 403 error.
insights.speaker: Suresh Gururajan
insights.so_what: A concrete pre-migration checklist item: verify SigV4a support on the exact service/endpoint type before architecting around it, since the debugging signal is opaque.
insights.quote: SigV4a support surface is narrower than you'd expect, and the failure mode when it's missing is a 403 that gives you nothing useful to diagnose.
insights.locator: A hard constraint to check first

insights.claim: The clearest signal that SigV4a would help a system is when clients run a pre-flight step whose only purpose is to learn which region to sign for -- that step exists solely because of the authentication model, not because it adds product value, and removing it is the actual payoff of migrating.
insights.speaker: Suresh Gururajan
insights.so_what: A generalizable diagnostic heuristic any team can apply to their own multi-region AWS architecture to decide if SigV4a migration is worth the coordination cost.
insights.quote: If your clients are running a pre-flight step whose only purpose is to determine which region to sign for, that's the signal.
